1:
"After these things the Lord appointed other seventy also, and sent them two and two before his face into every city and place, whither he himself would come." The witnesses dwell on the number seventy, the sending in pairs, and the order of the mission.

The number the fathers read as a sign of the nations. Cyril: "God had made known by the Prophets that the preaching of the Gospel... was to embrace not only Israel, but also the Gentile nations; and therefore after the twelve Apostles, there were other seventy-two also appointed." Bede: "to so many nations of the world was the Gospel to be preached." Cyril recalls the type in Moses, "who at the command of God chose out seventy, upon whom God poured out his Spirit" (cf. Numbers 11:16), and the twelve fountains and seventy palms at Elim. Augustine sees in the twelve "the order of Bishops" and in the seventy-two "the presbytery, the second order of priests."

The sending in pairs the fathers find meaningful. Gregory: "he sends the disciples to preach two and two, because there are two commands of charity, the love of God and love of our neighbour; and charity cannot exist without at least two" — "he who has not love to another ought not to undertake the office of preaching." Origen notes the ancient custom — "God led Israel out of Egypt by the hands of Moses and Aaron" — and "a brother assisted by a brother is as a fortified city."

"Before his face" marks the order of the work. Gregory: "the Lord follows his preachers, since the preaching comes first, and then the Lord enters into the tabernacle of our heart" — as Isaiah bids, "Prepare ye the way of the Lord" (cf. Isaiah 40:3). The witnesses thus see in the appointing of the seventy a widening of the gospel's reach beyond the Twelve, a foreshadowing of its mission to all nations — heralds sent two by two in love, going before the Lord to ready the towns for his own coming (cf. Matthew 10:1). Augustine adds a further figure: as "in twenty-four hours the whole world moves round and receives light", so "three times twenty-four makes seventy-two", hinting at "the mystery of enlightening the world by the Gospel of the Trinity" through the seventy-two whom Christ now sent.
2:
"Therefore said he unto them, The harvest truly is great, but the labourers are few: pray ye therefore the Lord of the harvest, that he would send forth labourers into his harvest." The witnesses dwell on the great harvest, the few labourers, and the prayer commanded.

The words repeat what Christ had said to the Twelve. Ellicott notes "the verses that follow contain... much in common with those spoken on the mission of the Twelve" (cf. Matthew 9:37), "an example of our Lord's repeating the expression of the same thoughts in nearly the same language." Robertson: "the need is the same and prayer is the answer in each case."

The harvest is great and ripe. The fields of souls are white and waiting (cf. John 4:35), but "the labourers are few" (cf. 1 Corinthians 3:9). Clarke draws out the figure: there is "an allusion here to the case of reapers, who, though the harvest was perfectly ripe, yet were in no hurry to cut it down" — the verb "send forth" (ekballē) meaning to thrust out, as the farmer hurries reapers to a crop "spoiling for want of being reaped."

The remedy is prayer. Wesley: "pray ye the Lord of the harvest, that he would thrust forth labourers — for God alone can do this; he alone can qualify and commission men for this work." Robertson: "prayer for preachers is Christ's method for increasing the supply." The fathers note that Christ, who bids them pray the Lord of the harvest, is himself that Lord — Cyril (on v. 1) and Chrysostom mark that "he then is the Lord of the harvest," who afterward sent the labourers he bade them ask for. The witnesses thus see the mission of the seventy framed by a sense of vast need and scant supply: the fields are ripe and the workers few, and the first duty of those sent is to pray for more — a charge laid on the whole Church, that God would thrust labourers into a harvest that is his own. Calvin notes the saying is repeated here for a fresh purpose — to stir the disciples to diligence by assuring them their labour "will not be fruitless, but that they will find, in abundance, opportunities of employment, and means of usefulness" in so ripe and ample a harvest.
3:
"Go your ways: behold, I send you forth as lambs among wolves." The witnesses dwell on the charge to courage, the lambs' innocence, and the conquering of the wolves.

The single word "Go" is a call to courage. Cyril of Alexandria likens Christ to a general before battle: "just as valiant generals... encourage those under their command bravely to resist the attacking foe... so does the Saviour send forth the disciples against the hosts of unbelievers." Chrysostom finds their comfort in the Sender: "Behold, I send you — as if he said, This will suffice for your consolation," the power of him who sends.

The "lambs" mark their character. Isidore of Pelusium: the name denotes "the simplicity and innocence in his disciples; for those who were riotous... he calls not lambs, but goats." Ambrose: "the disciples are appointed not to make prey, but to impart grace"; and the good Shepherd "has no fear of wolves for his flock," sending them as lambs "that that prophecy might be fulfilled, The wolf and the lamb shall feed together" (cf. Isaiah 11:6).

The wolves the fathers variously name — Bede, "the Scribes and Pharisees"; Ambrose, "the heretics," who "lurk about Christ's sheepfolds" and "attack the throat... while they seek the soul." Yet the issue is triumph, not destruction. Chrysostom: "this was a clear announcement of glorious triumph, that the disciples of Christ, when surrounded by their enemies as lambs among wolves, should still convert them" (cf. Matthew 10:16). The witnesses thus see Christ sending his heralds, defenceless and innocent, into a hostile world — not to prey but to bless, not to fear but to conquer. Their safety lies not in arms but in the Shepherd who sends them; and the marvel of the gospel is that such lambs, by meekness and grace, should overcome the wolves and bring them into the one fold. Gregory Nazianzen adds that Christ gave these charges "for the glory of the word, lest it should seem that enticements could more prevail over them"; the very defencelessness of the lambs was to make plain that their victory came from the truth they bore, not from any worldly advantage.
4:
"Carry neither purse, nor scrip, nor shoes: and salute no man by the way." The witnesses dwell on the freedom from worldly care and the haste the mission required.

The forbidding of purse, scrip, and shoes teaches dependence. Chrysostom (on v. 3) notes Christ "forbade all care about what is external to the body... nor did he allow men to take with them any of those things which were not attached to the body." The heralds were to travel light, trusting the Sender to provide, free of the encumbrances that would distract from their work.

"Salute no man by the way" the witnesses take of urgency, not discourtesy. Chrysostom: Christ "did not allow them to receive any distraction in their work, such as interruption by greetings"; "for it is a loss to waste the time which is fitter for preaching, in unnecessary things." The model is Elisha, who charged his servant, "if thou meet any man, salute him not" (cf. 2 Kings 4:29). Calvin (on v. 2) explains the Eastern salutation could consume much time, and Christ "bids them hasten forward, so as to pass by everything that would detain them" — not that they be "barbarous and uncivil," but swift. Ambrose adds Christ "did not forbid these things because the exercise of benevolence was displeasing to him, but because the motive of following after devotedness was more pleasing."

Lightfoot notes the special haste of this mission: the seventy were sent only to the towns "where Christ himself was to come in person," to "proclaim the coming of Christ there" — heralds running before the King. The witnesses thus see in these terse commands the marks of a true gospel errand: utter dependence on God for supply, freedom from worldly entanglement, and a single-minded haste that lets nothing — not even the courtesies of the road — delay the urgent announcement of the kingdom (cf. Matthew 10:10). Lightfoot distinguishes this haste from that of the Twelve: the seventy were not, like the apostles, to proclaim the kingdom wherever they met men, but only "to give notice that the Messiah was coming, and that in those places to which he was to come" — so they were not to be detained by greetings on the road.
5:
"And into whatsoever house ye enter, first say, Peace be to this house." The witnesses dwell on the gift of peace and its power.

Peace is to be the heralds' first word. Chrysostom: "Peace is the mother of all good things; without it all other things are vain. Our Lord therefore commanded his disciples on entering a house first to pronounce peace as a sign of good things." Ambrose: "that in truth we should convey the message of peace, and that our very first entrance be attended with the blessing of peace." So, Chrysostom adds, "he who presides in the Church gives it, saying, Peace to all."

The peace meant is full and many-sided. Chrysostom notes "holy men ask for peace, not only that which dwells among men in mutual intercourse, but that which belongs to ourselves; for oftentimes we wage war in our hearts." Titus of Bostra takes "Peace be to this house" as spoken "to them that dwell in the house" — "I speak to all, both the greater and the less."

The blessing is no empty form. Gregory (on the verses following) explains "the peace which is offered by the mouth of the preacher shall either rest on the house, if there be any one in it predestined to life... or, if no one be willing to hear, the preacher himself shall not be without fruit, for the peace returns to him, while the Lord gives him the recompense." Titus: "you indeed shall utter the word, but the blessing of peace shall be applied wherever I shall deem men worthy" (cf. Matthew 10:12). The witnesses thus see the gospel's greeting set at the threshold of every house: the heralds of Christ come not with the sword but with peace, and their first act is to pronounce upon those they visit the very blessing the gospel brings — a peace with God, within the soul, and among men, that is the sum of all good and the earnest of salvation (cf. John 14:27). Theophylact notes that the same peace, pronounced at the door, becomes the test of the house: where it is welcomed, the heralds remain; where it is refused, they shake off its dust and pass on, the offered grace neither forced nor lost.
6:
"And if the son of peace be there, your peace shall rest upon it; if not, it shall turn to you again." The witnesses dwell on the "son of peace" and the two outcomes of the blessing.

"Son of peace" is a Hebrew idiom. Clarke explains: "in the Jewish style, a man who has any good or bad quality is called the son of it" — so "the children of wisdom," "the children of wrath," "the son of perdition." Here "son of peace... means not only a peaceable, quiet man, but one also of good report for his uprightness and benevolence." Robertson agrees it "means one inclined to peace, describing the head of the household" (cf. Isaiah 9:6). Gill takes it of "any elect person... whom God has chosen to partake of peace by the blood of Christ."

Where such a one is found, the peace abides. Robertson notes the verb "shall rest upon" is a "double compound" meaning to settle down. Gregory (on v. 5) had explained the peace "shall rest on the house, if there be any one in it predestined to life, who follows the heavenly word which he hears."

Where none is found, the peace returns. Robertson: "the peace in that case will bend back with blessing upon the one who spoke it." The blessing, the witnesses stress, is never wasted. Gregory: "if no one be willing to hear, the preacher himself shall not be without fruit, for the peace returns to him, while the Lord gives him the recompense of reward for the labour of his work." The witnesses thus see the offered peace as a thing of real power, finding its proper resting-place in the worthy and recoiling in blessing upon the herald when rejected. The preacher's labour is secure either way: if his peace is received, the house is blessed; if refused, the blessing returns to him. So the messengers of Christ are taught that their work is never in vain — the grace they bring will light somewhere, on the hearer who receives it or back on themselves who bore it.
7:
"And in the same house remain, eating and drinking such things as they give: for the labourer is worthy of his hire. Go not from house to house." The witnesses dwell on the herald's maintenance and the command to stay put.

The labourer may live by his labour. Gregory (on v. 5) notes that "he who forbade them to carry purse and scrip allows them to be an expense to others, and to receive sustenance from preaching"; "now the very food which supports him is part of the wages of the labourer." Calvin: "you will be at liberty to live at the expense of others... for it is proper that those for whose benefit you labour should supply you with food." Theophylact: Christ "taught his disciples to beg, and wished them to receive their nourishment as a reward."

"The labourer is worthy of his hire" the commentators note is cited by Paul as Scripture. Ellicott and Robertson observe that 1 Timothy 5:18 quotes these very words "as a citation from the Scripture" (cf. 1 Corinthians 9:14). Ellicott draws the inference that Paul, "during his long companionship with St. Luke," knew "the materials which the Evangelist was collecting," so that "the Gospel of St. Luke must have been, in part at least, written and recognised" by then.

"Go not from house to house" guards against waste and distraction. Robertson: "as a habit... avoid waste of time with such rounds of invitations." Barnes, with Thomson, explains the Eastern custom by which each neighbour in turn must invite the stranger, a system "involving much ostentation and hypocrisy," consuming time and counteracting "the success of a spiritual mission." The witnesses thus see Christ providing wisely for his heralds: they are to accept, without scruple or restlessness, the hospitality of the worthy house that receives them, content with what is set before them, neither anxious about provision nor wandering from table to table — that nothing might distract them from the one work for which they were sent (cf. Matthew 10:10). Barnes, citing Thomson, notes the Eastern etiquette that obliged a stranger to eat in turn with each neighbour, a custom that "consumes much time, causes unusual distraction of mind, leads to levity, and every way counteracts the success of a spiritual mission" — which the command to stay in one house was meant to prevent.
8:
"And into whatsoever city ye enter, and they receive you, eat such things as are set before you," The witnesses dwell on the gospel liberty granted and the lesson of contentment.

The precept frees the disciples from rabbinic scruple. Lightfoot notes how "precise and curious" the traditional canons were "about not eating unless what were clean, what had been duly tithed, and from which the Trumah had been duly separated" — so burdensome that "the strict traditionists should not be famished" was almost a wonder. "From the irksomeness and perplexity of this niceness," he says, "doth our Saviour acquit and absolve his followers, partly that he might introduce the gospel liberty, partly also consulting the necessity of his disciples," whose lot it was to lodge "amongst persons perfectly unknown to them" (cf. 1 Corinthians 10:27).

Ellicott notes the form of the precept is "peculiar to St. Luke," though its spirit matches the charge to the Twelve: "the Evangelist preachers were to accept whatever was provided for them by a willing host, and to avoid even the appearance of caring for outward comforts."

Robertson presses the practical point. "Such things as are set before you" — "the things placed before you from time to time" — teach "this lesson of common politeness," which "every preacher needs." "These directions may seem perfunctory and even commonplace, but every teacher of young preachers knows how necessary they are." The witnesses thus see Christ providing for his heralds a double freedom: liberty from the legal niceties that would have hampered their mission, and freedom from anxiety about fare. They were to eat thankfully whatever a hospitable house set before them, neither fastidious about ceremonial purity nor covetous of comfort — content with what was given, that their whole care might be for the message they bore (cf. Matthew 10:11). Gill notes the precept presupposes a city that receives them; where welcome was given, the disciples were to settle and accept its hospitality freely, neither standing on ceremony nor giving offence by a fastidious refusal of what their hosts had gladly provided. The same lesson, Robertson notes, was given both to the Twelve and to the Seventy, a sign of how easily even good men stumble over small matters of courtesy and contentment that can mar a spiritual mission.
9:
"And heal the sick that are therein, and say unto them, The kingdom of God is come nigh unto you." The witnesses dwell on the works of healing and the message of the kingdom.

The heralds were to heal as well as preach. Gill notes this "would not only show their power and warrant, their mission and commission, but also their goodness and beneficence to men, and would be a sufficient return for what they ate and drank." The miracles authenticated the messengers and commended their word.

Their message was the kingdom. "Say unto them, The kingdom of God is come nigh unto you" — Clarke notes this "was the general text on which they were to preach all their sermons," the same proclamation made by the Baptist and by Christ (cf. Matthew 3:2). Ellicott: "this was to be the burden of those who, as preachers, were, in the strictest sense, the heralds of the great King." Robertson notes the perfect tense "is come nigh" (ēngiken eph' hymas) — the kingdom has drawn near and presses upon them.

The witnesses see the healing and the heralding joined as one work. The cures were not ends in themselves but signs that the kingdom had indeed come near, the power of heaven breaking visibly into the towns the seventy entered. Where the sick were healed, the word "the kingdom of God is come nigh" was no empty claim but a fact men could see. So the messengers came with both deed and word — relieving the body to open the ear, and announcing that in the One whose forerunners they were, the long-promised reign of God had arrived at men's very doors. The nearness of the kingdom was the heart of their gospel: not a distant hope but a present reality, come nigh to all who would receive it (cf. Matthew 10:7). Robertson observes that the seventy, like the Twelve, were given a single unchanging text — the nearness of the kingdom — so that wherever they went the burden was the same: the reign of God had drawn near in the One whose coming they announced, and men must reckon with it.
10:
"But into whatsoever city ye enter, and they receive you not, go your ways out into the streets of the same, and say," The witnesses dwell on the rejected city and the solemn public witness.

Christ provides also for refusal. Gill notes the Lord "here directs how to behave towards other cities they should come to, and be rejected." Where one city received the heralds and was blessed, another would shut its doors; and for that case too there was a divine instruction.

The rejected messengers were to go public. Robertson notes they were to go "into the broad open streets" — "out of the inhospitable houses" into "the streets thereof," there to make their declaration openly. The witness against the city was not to be whispered in a corner but proclaimed where all could hear.

The act that followed (in the next verse) the witnesses read with Chrysostom (on v. 13), who warns that the sentence falls "not upon them alone, but upon us also... if we receive not the guests who come to us, since he commanded them to shake off the very dust from their feet." The verse thus marks the turning of the mission from blessing to testimony. The heralds came offering peace and the kingdom; but where their offer was spurned, they did not slink away in silence. They stood in the open streets and pronounced, before the whole city, both the guilt of its refusal and the nearness of the kingdom it had rejected. The witnesses see in this the gravity of the gospel offer: to reject the messengers of Christ is no small or private thing, but draws down a public and solemn witness, leaving the city without excuse before God for the mercy it had thrust away. Gill notes the contrast is deliberate: having told them how to act where they were received, Christ now directs their conduct "towards other cities they should come to, and be rejected", so that no case should find his heralds without guidance, whether welcomed or spurned. Robertson notes the heralds were to move "out of the inhospitable houses into the broad open streets", making their declaration not in private resentment but as a public and deliberate testimony before the whole city that had closed its doors to the gospel.
11:
"Even the very dust of your city, which cleaveth on us, we do wipe off against you: notwithstanding be ye sure of this, that the kingdom of God is come nigh unto you." The witnesses dwell on the shaking off of the dust and the message that stands even in rejection.

The gesture is a sign of total separation. Robertson notes the dust that "cleaveth" (kollēthenta, clinging "as dust and mud do to shoes") was to be wiped off "against you" — "a fine example of the dative of disadvantage." "Dust is a plague in the east," he adds; "hence the orientals took off the sandals on entering a house." To shake off even the dust of the city (cf. Luke 9:5; Acts 13:51) was to renounce all fellowship with it, leaving nothing of it upon them, a witness that they would carry away not the least particle of a place that had refused the gospel.

Yet the message stands unchanged. "Notwithstanding be ye sure of this, that the kingdom of God is come nigh unto you." Ellicott finds "something very solemnly impressive in the fact that this is the message to be uttered alike to the believing and the unbelieving"; "now, as of old, the prophets of the Lord had to utter their proclamation, whether men would hear or whether they would forbear" (cf. Ezekiel 2:7).

The witnesses thus see in the verse the double edge of the gospel. The same kingdom that comes near as blessing to the believing comes near as judgment to the rejecting. The city that would not receive it cannot make it untrue: the kingdom had drawn near to them, and their refusal did not push it away but only sealed their guilt. The shaken dust testifies that the heralds owe them nothing further; the unaltered word testifies that the kingdom was really offered, and really spurned. So the gospel, when refused, becomes a witness against the refuser — for the nearness of God's reign, once announced, remains a fact, whether men welcome or reject it. Adam Clarke refers the gesture to the parallel charge to the Twelve, where the shaking of the dust is a public renunciation; the act, though silent, spoke louder than words, declaring the city wholly given over to the consequences of its own refusal.
12:
"But I say unto you, that it shall be more tolerable in that day for Sodom, than for that city." The witnesses dwell on the dreadful comparison and the principle of proportionate judgment.

The same warning was given to the Twelve. Gill notes it is "the same that he said to the twelve apostles when he sent them out" (cf. Matthew 10:15). Robertson marks the comparative "more tolerable" (anektoteron), "the comparative of the verbal adjective" used in this phrase through the Gospels.

The comparison is with Sodom — the byword of wickedness, destroyed by fire from heaven (cf. Genesis 19:24). Yet Christ declares that "in that day," the day of judgment, it shall fare better with Sodom than with the city that rejected his heralds. The reason lies in the measure of light. Sodom sinned grievously, but had not seen the works nor heard the words that the cities of Galilee enjoyed; the rejecting city sins against far greater mercy, and so against far greater knowledge.

The witnesses thus see in the saying the solemn principle that runs through Scripture: judgment is in proportion to privilege. To have the gospel offered, the kingdom announced, the messengers of Christ at one's door, and to refuse all this, is a guilt that outweighs even the foul sins of Sodom. The greater the mercy spurned, the heavier the doom. The verse stands therefore as a warning not only to those ancient towns but to every place and soul that has heard the gospel: it is a more dreadful thing to reject the light of Christ than to have perished in the darkness of Sodom, for to whom much is given, of him shall much be required, and the day of judgment will reckon with men according to the mercy they received and the use they made of it. Ellicott notes the phrase "in that day" fixes the reference firmly upon the final judgment, when the secret measure of every man's light and refusal shall be opened, and the cities that scorned the gospel shall be judged by a stricter rule than Sodom ever knew.
13:
"Woe unto thee, Chorazin! woe unto thee, Bethsaida! for if the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon, which have been done in you, they had a great while ago repented, sitting in sackcloth and ashes." The witnesses dwell on the woe, the greater guilt of the privileged, and the meaning of the mourning.

The woe falls on cities that saw and believed not. Ambrose: "our Lord warns us that they will meet with a heavier punishment who have refused to follow the Gospel than those who have chosen to break the law." Bede names them — Chorazin, Bethsaida, and Capernaum — "cities of Galilee situated on the shore of the lake," and notes they "are worse than the Gentiles who break through the law of nature only, seeing that after despising the written law, they feared not to despise also the Son of God and his glory."

Tyre and Sidon, the heathen cities, would have done better. "If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon... they had a great while ago repented" (cf. Matthew 11:21). Bede unfolds the signs of repentance: "by sackcloth, which is woven from the hairs of goats, he signifies a sharp remembrance of previous sin; by ashes he hints at the consideration of death, by which we are reduced to dust; by the sitting down, he implies the lowliness of our conscience" (cf. Jonah 3:6).

The woe is spoken in mourning, for our sake. Chrysostom: "our Lord mourns over these cities for our example, because shedding tears... over those who are insensible to grief is no slight antidote"; and "he draws them over to what is good, not only by lamenting over them, but also by alarming them." The witnesses thus see Christ's lament as both judgment and mercy: he pronounces woe on the towns that wasted their unequalled privilege, yet pronounces it with tears, that the warning might still turn some to repentance. The greater the light, the greater the guilt of refusing it — and the heathen who never saw such works will rise in judgment against the favoured cities that did and would not repent.
14:
"But it shall be more tolerable for Tyre and Sidon at the judgment, than for you." The witnesses dwell on the comparison applied and the warning it carries.

The sentence repeats, for the Galilean cities, the principle already stated of Sodom (cf. Matthew 11:22). Tyre and Sidon, the great heathen ports long under the prophets' censure (cf. Ezekiel 28:21), will find the day of judgment more bearable than Chorazin and Bethsaida. The reason is the same: the measure of light received.

The heathen cities had idolatry and wickedness, but they had not seen the mighty works of Christ. The cities of Galilee had the Son of God himself among them, working miracles and preaching the kingdom, and they repented not. Their guilt is therefore greater, and their doom heavier, than that of the pagans who never had such opportunity. The comparison strips away every excuse: it is not the heathen darkness but the despised gospel light that brings the sorest condemnation.

The witnesses press the warning home upon the reader. Chrysostom had noted (on v. 13) that this sentence falls "not upon them alone, but upon us also." Every place and every soul that has heard the gospel and seen its power stands in the position of Chorazin and Bethsaida. To have the means of grace and neglect them, to know the truth and refuse it, is to lay up a judgment more terrible than that of the most notorious sinners who never knew it. The day of judgment, the witnesses observe, will not reckon by the bare catalogue of sins but by the light against which they were committed — and by that reckoning the favoured but impenitent will fare worse than Tyre and Sidon, worse even than Sodom. Gill notes the Syriac and other versions read "in the day of judgment", fixing the comparison upon that final reckoning; the heathen ports, judged for sins against nature only, shall stand in less condemnation than the towns that sinned against the very presence of the Son of God. Chrysostom had pressed (on v. 13) that this judgment touches not those ancient cities alone but every hearer since, so that the warning reaches down to all who, having the gospel near them, treat it with the same neglect.
15:
"And thou, Capernaum, which art exalted to heaven, shalt be thrust down to hell." The witnesses dwell on Capernaum's exaltation, its fall, and the lesson of privilege abused.

Capernaum was lifted high. Gill notes the exaltation may refer to its situation, "on a very high hill," or to "its privileges, through the ministry and miracles of Christ," or to "the pride and loftiness of the inhabitants," elated with their favoured lot — "a famous port... in an excellent temperament of the air." Bede (on v. 13) takes it of the honour Christ's presence gave: Capernaum was "exalted to heaven by my dwelling in you, and by my miracles."

Its fall would be as deep as its height. "Shalt be thrust down to hell" — Robertson notes the question form (mē hypsōthēsē) "expects the answer No," and "unto Hades" sets it "in contrast to Heaven, as in Isaiah" (cf. Isaiah 14:13–15; Ezekiel 26:20). Bede offers two senses: either she is cast down "because thou proudly resisted my preaching," having "raised herself up to heaven by her pride"; or, "because thou art exalted to heaven by my dwelling... thou shalt be beaten with more stripes, since even these thou refused to believe" (cf. Matthew 11:23).

The witnesses note the prophecy's fulfilment and one telling omission. Robertson: "the desolation of the whole neighbourhood, and the difficulty of identifying even the site of these flourishing towns, is part of the fulfilment of this prophecy." He marks too that Nazareth is absent from the list — wondering whether "the tender memories of boyhood... keep from his lips the name of the arch-rejector." The witnesses thus see in Capernaum the supreme warning of the passage: the city most favoured, the very seat of Christ's Galilean ministry, becomes by its unbelief the city most deeply doomed. Privilege unimproved does not merely fail to save; it sinks the soul lower than it found it, for the higher the exaltation in grace, the deeper the fall in judgment. Robertson notes the contrast of heaven and Hades is drawn straight from Isaiah's taunt over the king of Babylon, who said "I will ascend into heaven" and was brought down to the pit; Capernaum, lifted by grace, is warned of the same reversal that pride and unbelief always invite.
16:
"He that heareth you heareth me; and he that despiseth you despiseth me; and he that despiseth me despiseth him that sent me." The witnesses dwell on the dignity of the messengers and the weight of receiving or rejecting them.

The word is first an encouragement. Gill notes it was "said for the encouragement of the seventy disciples, that though they would be rejected by some, they would be received by others," who would embrace their doctrine "as if the Messiah himself had been among them." Christ "considers them as ambassadors in his name... taking what is done to them as done to himself."

The principle binds the messengers to Christ and to the Father. Cyril (on v. 13): "whatever is said by the holy Apostles must be received, since he who hears them hears Christ"; and Bede, that "every one... on hearing or despising the preaching of the Gospel might learn that he is not despising or hearing" the men only, but Christ in them (cf. Matthew 10:40; John 13:20). Clarke cites the rabbinic parallel: "a man's ambassador is like to himself"; to honour the precepts is to honour God, to despise them is to despise him (cf. John 5:23).

Calvin guards the saying's true scope. It is "a mistake to suppose that this passage is a repetition" of the earlier word to the Twelve, which spoke of "offices of kindness"; here Christ "recommends faith, which receives God in his Word" — the matter is "not of persons, but of doctrine." Robertson notes the verb "rejecteth" (atheteō, "to set aside") makes "it so grave a responsibility to be co-workers with Christ." The witnesses thus see the seventy sent out clothed with their Master's own authority: to hear them is to hear Christ, and through Christ the Father; to despise them is to despise both. The dignity is unspeakable and the danger extreme — for the response men make to the lowly heralds of the gospel is, in truth, the response they make to God himself. Robertson notes the same verb for "reject" is used earlier of the scribes and Pharisees who "set aside" the counsel of God against themselves; to set aside the messengers of Christ is to set aside, in the end, the saving purpose of God himself.
17:
"And the seventy returned again with joy, saying, Lord, even the devils are subject unto us through thy name." The witnesses dwell on the disciples' joy, the power they confess, and the right object of rejoicing.

The seventy come back exulting. Cyril notes that, "made ministers of preaching, they received power over the unclean spirits," and now, "when they returned, they confess the power of him who honoured them." Their joy is real, but the fathers gently correct its aim. Cyril: "they seemed indeed to rejoice rather that they were made workers of miracles than that they had become ministers of preaching. But they had better have rejoiced in those whom they had taken, as St. Paul says... My joy and my crown" (cf. Philippians 4:1).

What they confess is power "through thy name." The devils were subject not to any virtue of their own but to the name of Christ in which they spoke (cf. Mark 16:17). The very wording of their report — "subject unto us through thy name" — owns that the authority was borrowed, the triumph their Master's.

The witnesses thus see in the return of the seventy a scene of glad success, tempered by a coming word of caution. The mission had proved Christ's power: even the demons fled at his name on the lips of his lowly heralds. Yet the disciples' delight in this visible mastery, natural as it was, needed to be raised higher — from rejoicing in the gifts to rejoicing in the Giver, and from the power over spirits to the salvation of souls and their own names written in heaven, as Christ would presently teach them. Their joy was good, but there was a better joy still, and the Lord would not let them rest in the lesser. Cyril adds the alternative reading of the verse, that "I saw Satan as lightning fall from heaven" tells how the adversary, who "before the coming of our Saviour had subdued the world to him and was worshipped by all men", fell "from the highest power to the lowest impotence", trodden under foot by those who now worship Christ.
18:
"And he said unto them, I beheld Satan as lightning fall from heaven." The witnesses dwell on the vision, its timing, and the suddenness of Satan's fall.

Christ answers their joy with a greater wonder. Gregory notes the word was spoken "in order to put down high thoughts in the hearts of his disciples": "himself related the account of the fall which the teacher of pride suffered, that they might learn by the example of the author of pride what they would have to dread from the sin of pride."

The "lightning" marks the fall as sudden and headlong. Bede: by it "he signifies either a fall headlong from the high places to the lowest, or that, now cast down, he transforms himself into an angel of light." Robertson presses the grammar: the imperfect "I was beholding" with the aorist "fall" pictures "the victory of the Seventy over the demons... a flash of lightning." Ellicott: "while they were working, their Master had been following them in spirit... their triumph over the demons was the beginning and earnest of a final conquest over Satan" (cf. Revelation 12:9).

The witnesses connect the vision with the dawning of the kingdom. Lightfoot reads it of the present hour: "when I sent you forth I saw Satan's fall at hand"; for "when the Messiah had determined to exhibit himself... it could not otherwise be but that the power of Satan should sink." Barnes links the imagery to the casting-out of demons: "I saw at your command devils immediately depart, as quick as the flash of lightning." The witnesses thus see Christ lifting the disciples' eyes from their small skirmishes to the great war: their conquest of the demons was no isolated marvel but the visible sign of Satan's overthrow, the usurper dethroned from his high places (cf. John 12:31) as the kingdom of God advanced. And the lesson against pride is plain — for it was by pride that the shining one fell, and his ruin is the warning of all who exalt themselves. Basil distinguishes the names: he is "Satan", which signifies an enemy to God, and "the Devil", the accuser who assists us in doing evil; an angelic nature not holy by essence but by participation, who fell because, unlike God, he was by nature susceptible of evil.
19:
"Behold, I give unto you power to tread on serpents and scorpions, and over all the power of the enemy: and nothing shall by any means hurt you." The witnesses dwell on the authority given and its true scope.

The serpents and scorpions the witnesses read both literally and figuratively. Gill takes them "figuratively of the devil, and his principalities and powers." Bede distinguishes: "serpents who bite with the teeth" signify foes "raging openly," "scorpions who sting with the tail" those "plotting in secret"; Theophylact, the open sins of "adultery and murder" against "the sins of the spirit." A literal sense is not excluded — Bede recalls that "Paul, when attacked by a viper, suffered no injury" (cf. Acts 28:5; Psalm 91:13).

"Over all the power of the enemy" is the heart of the gift. Chrysostom notes Christ "added, And over all the power of the enemy," lest "we should suppose this was spoken of beasts." The authority reaches to Satan himself and all his agents.

The kernel, Ellicott urges, lies in the last words. He warns against a "merely literal interpretation," which would make "treading on serpents a greater work than casting out demons"; rather "the serpent and the scorpion are symbols of spiritual powers of evil," and "the real kernel of the promise lies in Nothing shall by any means hurt you" — which "find their interpretation in the thought that nothing shall separate us from the love of God, and that all things work together for good" (cf. Romans 16:20). The witnesses thus see Christ arming his messengers for the whole spiritual war. The power he gives is over Satan and every evil that serves him, open or hidden; and the promise is not that they shall meet no danger, but that no danger shall finally harm them — kept, amid serpents and scorpions, by the One who has already seen the enemy fall. Bede notes that the literal preservation was really fulfilled — Paul shook the viper into the fire unhurt, and tradition tells how John drank poison without harm — yet these stand as the visible pledge of the deeper safety, that no spiritual venom of the enemy can finally destroy the servant of Christ.
20:
"Notwithstanding in this rejoice not, that the spirits are subject unto you; but rather rejoice, because your names are written in heaven." The witnesses dwell on the higher joy Christ commends.

Christ redirects their gladness. Barnes: "though it was an honour to work miracles... yet it is a subject of chief joy that we are numbered among the people of God, and have a title to everlasting life." The power over spirits, great as it was, must not be the ground of their deepest rejoicing; that lay in their own salvation.

"Names written in heaven" the witnesses explain from the civic register. Clarke: "the ancient custom of writing the names of all the citizens in a public register"; to be so enrolled — as in "the book of life" (cf. Philippians 4:3; Revelation 3:5) — meant to be a citizen of heaven, "friends of God... approved by him." Robertson notes the perfect tense, "stand written, enrolled."

Whether such enrolment could be forfeited the witnesses weigh. Ellicott notes "the words leave open the question," but "the current language of the Old Testament — the prayer of Moses, Blot me out of thy book (cf. Exodus 32:32), the warnings of Exodus 32:33" — and the conditioned promise of Revelation 3:5 "would suggest the thought that even here the joy was to be tempered with fear and trembling." The witnesses thus see Christ teaching the true measure of blessedness. Gifts and powers, however dazzling, are not the believer's chief treasure; for one may cast out devils and yet be lost. The deepest and safest joy is to be God's own, enrolled among the citizens of heaven — a joy that no failure of miracles can dim, and which the lowliest believer shares as fully as the most gifted apostle. Clarke adds that the same register from which the worthy are enrolled is that from which the unworthy were blotted out, so that to have one's name written in heaven is to be numbered, by grace, among the living citizens of God's city — a dignity infinitely beyond the power to command spirits.
21:
"In that hour Jesus rejoiced in spirit, and said, I thank thee, O Father, Lord of heaven and earth, that thou hast hid these things from the wise and prudent, and hast revealed them unto babes." The witnesses dwell on Christ's joy, his thanksgiving, and the babes to whom revelation comes.

Christ rejoices over the harvest of grace. Theophylact: "as a loving father rejoices to see his sons do right, so Christ also rejoices that his Apostles were made worthy of such good things." Cyril: he "rejoiced in the Holy Spirit, that is, in the results which came forth through the Holy Spirit" — "the conversion of sinners." His "I thank thee," Bede notes, is the language not of penitence but of thanksgiving, "as frequently in the Psalms."

The "wise and prudent" are the self-wise; the "babes," the humble. Ambrose: a babe is "one who knew nothing of exalting himself, and of boasting in proud words of the excellence of his wisdom, as the Pharisees often do." Bede: "to the wise and prudent he opposed not the dull and foolish, but babes — that is, the humble — to show that he condemned pride, not quickness of mind" (cf. 1 Corinthians 1:27; Matthew 11:25).

The thanksgiving guards Christ's deity even as he prays. Cyril answers "they whose hearts are perverted," who say "the Son gives thanks to the Father as being inferior": "what should prevent the Son of the same substance with the Father from praising his own Father?" — and he calls him "Father, and the Lord of heaven and earth." Epiphanius notes that Marcion's mutilated text omits "heaven and earth... lest it should be supposed that he calls the Father the Creator." The witnesses thus see Christ, in the hour of the seventy's return, breaking into a prayer of holy joy: he blesses the Father's sovereign good pleasure, which hides the mysteries of the kingdom from the proud and self-sufficient and reveals them to the lowly — and in the very act of thanksgiving owns the Father as Lord of all, and himself as the Son who shares his nature and his works.
22:
"All things are delivered to me of my Father: and no man knoweth who the Son is, but the Father; and who the Father is, but the Son, and he to whom the Son will reveal him." The witnesses dwell on the Son's supreme authority and the mutual knowledge of Father and Son.

"All things are delivered to me" asserts a universal authority. Robertson notes the timeless aorist ("delivered") marks the Son's "possession of all power from the Father." (Clarke and Ellicott note the marginal "and turning to his disciples" here is "a transcriber's error," belonging to the next verse.)

The mutual knowledge the witnesses find astonishing in the Synoptics. Robertson calls it "an aerolite from the Johannean heaven" — "the same use and contrast of the Father, the Son, in both Matthew and Luke." "No sane criticism can get rid of this Johannine bit," he insists; "it is idle to try to whittle away... the high claims made by Jesus." It is "an ecstatic prayer... on terms of perfect equality and understanding between the Father and the Son" (cf. John 17:25; Matthew 11:27).

The Son reveals the Father sovereignly. Robertson notes "the Son claims the power to reveal the Father to whomsoever he wills" — "divine sovereignty most assuredly." Cyril of Jerusalem adds that even "the Angels behold as much as they can bear," but "the Only-begotten Son, together with the Holy Ghost, knoweth the Father fully," and "according as each can bear, revealeth God through the Spirit." Wesley sums: "who the Son is — essentially one with the Father." The witnesses thus see in this verse one of the loftiest claims of Christ in all the Gospels: he holds all things from the Father, shares with him an exclusive mutual knowledge that none but they possess, and alone can unveil the Father to men. Far from contradicting his thanksgiving, it grounds it — for the babes to whom the Father is revealed are those to whom the Son, in his sovereign grace, has willed to make him known (cf. John 3:35). Robertson stresses that this passage, far older than the Fourth Gospel, already shows "the Messianic consciousness in complete control and with perfect confidence in the outcome", giving us a glimpse of the communion the Son enjoyed with the Father in those whole nights of prayer upon the mountain.
23:
"And he turned him unto his disciples, and said privately, Blessed are the eyes which see the things that ye see:" The witnesses dwell on the turning to the disciples and the blessedness of their sight.

Christ turns from the multitude to bless his own. Theophylact: "having said... No one knoweth who the Father is but the Son, and to whomsoever the Son will reveal him, he pronounces a blessing upon his disciples, to whom the Father was revealed through him." Cyril of Alexandria: "he turns to them, since he rejected the Jews, who were deaf, with their understandings blinded... and gives himself wholly to those who love him."

The "seeing" meant is the sight of faith. Cyril explains "seeing does not signify the action of the eyes, but the pleasure which the mind receives" — for "many Jews have seen Christ performing divine works... with their bodily sight, yet all were not fitted to receive the blessing, for they believed not." "Blessed then are our eyes, since we see by faith the Word who is made man for us, shedding upon us the glory of his Godhead" (cf. Matthew 13:16).

The blessing belongs to the gospel age. The disciples saw what generations had longed for: the Christ present, the kingdom come, the Father revealed in the Son. The witnesses thus see Christ pronouncing his followers happy beyond the saints of old — not for any merit, but for the privilege of their hour. To behold, with the eye of faith, the incarnate Word and his works, was a blessedness the prophets and kings desired but did not attain. The very things the seventy had been sent to proclaim, and now rejoiced to have wrought, were the open manifestation of what the ancients had seen only afar; and to have eyes that truly saw it — that perceived in Jesus the glory of God — was the highest privilege a soul could know. Bede adds that the prophets, "looking afar off, saw him in a glass and darkly", but the apostles, "having our Lord present with them, whatever things they wished to learn had no need to be taught by angels or any other kind of vision" — the immediacy of their sight being itself the measure of their blessedness.
24:
"For I tell you, that many prophets and kings have desired to see those things which ye see, and have not seen them; and to hear those things which ye hear, and have not heard them." The witnesses dwell on the longing of the ancients and the superiority of the disciples' lot.

The prophets and kings are the saints of old. Gill names "Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, who were prophets... and David, Solomon"; Ellicott notes Matthew has "prophets and righteous men" (cf. 1 Peter 1:10). They "desired" what the disciples now enjoyed — the presence and work of the Christ.

That they desired it the witnesses take as proof they knew of him. Chrysostom (on v. 23): "from this saying many imagine that the prophets were without the knowledge of Christ. But... no one desires what he has no conception of; they therefore knew the Son of God." Yet they saw only from afar. Bede: "those looking afar off saw him in a glass and darkly, but the Apostles, having our Lord present with them," needed no such mediated vision (cf. John 8:56, Abraham "saw my day and was glad"; Hebrews 11:13).

Calvin draws the comparison of the two ages. "The condition of the Church at the present day is justly pronounced to be preferable to that of the holy fathers... because to them was exhibited, under shadows and figures only, what is now openly manifested in the shining face of Christ." Yet the fathers "were satisfied with their lot, and enjoyed a blessed peace," even while their desires "extended farther" — like Simeon, they "breathed after Christ like hungry persons, and yet possessed a serene faith." The witnesses thus see Christ measuring the disciples' blessedness against the longing of the greatest of the old covenant. Prophets and kings, who knew Christ only in promise and shadow, yearned to see what the disciples beheld in the flesh. To live in the day of fulfilment, with the Saviour present and the kingdom come, was a privilege the holiest of former ages desired but were not granted — and which the disciples, and every believer since, possess in the open revelation of the gospel.
25:
"And, behold, a certain lawyer stood up, and tempted him, saying, Master, what shall I do to inherit eternal life?" The witnesses dwell on the lawyer's motive, his title, and the question of "doing."

The lawyer came to test, not to learn. Bede thinks "the lawyer took occasion of tempting our Lord" from the saying that the disciples' "names were written in heaven." Cyril sets it among the campaigns against Christ: "there were certain men who then went about... bringing charges against Christ, and saying that he spoke of the commands of Moses as useless"; so "a lawyer, wishing to entrap Christ into saying something against Moses, comes and tempts him, calling him Master, though not bearing to be his disciple."

The question concerns "doing." "What shall I do to inherit eternal life?" — the language, Cyril notes, the lawyer "adopts" because Christ "was wont to speak... concerning eternal life." It is the question of one who trusts his own works (cf. Matthew 19:16).

Christ meets him on his own ground — the law. Ambrose notes the lawyer "was one of those who think themselves skilled in the law, and who keep the letter of the law while they know nothing of its spirit"; and "from a part of the law itself our Lord proves them to be ignorant of the law." The witnesses thus see the encounter open as a duel of wits: a professional interpreter of the law, hostile and self-confident, comes to trap the Teacher with a question about eternal life. Christ neither rebuffs nor flatters him, but turns him back to the very law he professed to master — beginning a dialogue that will expose, beneath the lawyer's learning, both his ignorance of love's true scope and his need of a righteousness greater than his own (cf. Matthew 22:35). Maximus notes that the law required a threefold love of God precisely "that it might pluck us away from the threefold fashion of the world, as touching possessions, glory, and pleasure" — the very three in which Christ himself was tempted; the lawyer's question opened the way to that searching demand.
26:
"He said unto him, What is written in the law? how readest thou?" The witnesses dwell on Christ's referral to the law and its purpose.

Christ sends the lawyer to his own province. Gill notes the referral was "with great propriety," "partly because this man, by his office and character, was an interpreter of the law; and partly because his question was, What shall I do?" Robertson: "as a lawyer it was his business to know the facts in the law and the proper interpretation"; "the rabbis had a formula, What readest thou?" Lightfoot confirms it was "an expression very common in the schools" when a text was brought in proof, noting Christ's difference is that he "calls to another to allege some text," where the schoolmen would cite it themselves.

The purpose was to expose the lawyer's need. Barnes: "the lawyer was doubtless endeavouring to justify himself by obeying the law. He trusted to his own works." So Christ "showed him what the law required, and thus showed him that he needed a better righteousness than his own. This is the proper use of the law" — "the law becomes a schoolmaster to lead us to him" (cf. Galatians 3:24).

Calvin guards the answer's logic. Christ "speaks here about obtaining salvation, in agreement with the question" — not "absolutely how men may arrive at eternal life, but how they ought to live, in order to be accounted righteous." The law "does not falsely hold out salvation to its followers, if any one fully observed all that it commands" (cf. Romans 3:20). The witnesses thus see Christ's counter-question as a piece of profound wisdom. He does not argue with the lawyer but sends him to the text, knowing that the law, rightly read, will both state the perfect standard and expose the man's failure to reach it. The law is no false guide; it shows the way of life by works — but in showing it, it convinces the honest soul of its own inability, and so drives it to seek a righteousness beyond the law's reach. Calvin adds that this manner of teaching — God first demanding the righteousness of works, and then offering a gratuitous righteousness without works — is no contradiction; "it is necessary that men should be convinced of their righteous condemnation, that they may betake themselves to the mercy of God."
27:
"And he answering said, Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy strength, and with all thy mind; and thy neighbour as thyself." The witnesses dwell on the lawyer's summary of the law and the totality of love it demands.

The lawyer answers well, joining two commands. The first is from Deuteronomy 6:5 — words, Gill and Robertson note, "written on the phylacteries" and recited daily; the second from Leviticus 19:18 (cf. Matthew 22:37). Robertson: "the lawyer knew the law," summing it as Jesus himself elsewhere does.

The four powers name the whole man. Gregory of Nyssa reads them of the soul's faculties: "by the heart he signified the bodily substance... by the soul the sensitive; but by the mind, the higher nature, the intellectual or reflective faculty." Wesley: "the heart, a general expression," explained by loving "with the warmest affection... the most vigorous efforts of thy will... and in the most wise and reasonable manner thou canst." Lightfoot notes "with all thy mind" is not in Moses' Hebrew exactly, the clause drawn out in fuller form.

The love demanded admits no rival. Basil (on v. 25): "by saying with all thy mind, he does not admit of any division of love to other things; for whatever love you cast on lower things necessarily takes away from the whole" — as a full vessel is diminished by whatever flows out, "so also the soul, whatever love it has wasted upon things unlawful, has so much lessened its love to God." The witnesses thus see the law's whole moral demand gathered into two commands of love — God with the entire being, the neighbour as oneself. The standard is total: every faculty of the soul bent wholly upon God, leaving nothing for any rival affection. It is a summary the lawyer could recite, but, as the sequel will show, one whose searching height he had not begun to measure against his own heart. Robertson notes there is no difficulty in this incident standing beside the later one in the temple (Mark 12:28-34), where a scribe gives a like summary and Jesus commends him; the law's two great commands were a recognised summary, which both the friendly scribe and this tempting lawyer could rightly recite.
28:
"And he said unto him, Thou hast answered right: this do, and thou shalt live." The witnesses dwell on the approval, the command, and its hidden difficulty.

Christ accepts the answer as far as it goes. Robertson: "the answer was correct so far as the words went." Ellicott notes "the acceptance of the lawyer's answer as theoretically true was part of the method of our Lord's teaching."

"This do, and thou shalt live" the witnesses take as solemn truth, not irony. Wesley: "here is no irony, but a deep and weighty truth. He, and he alone, shall live for ever, who thus loves God and his neighbour." Ellicott: "love was really life, at once its source and its manifestation, if only the love were true, and the test of its being true was action."

Yet the command hides an impossibility. Robertson: "there was only one trouble with the lawyer's answer. No one ever did or ever can do what the law lays down towards God and man always. To slip once is to fail." Calvin reconciles this with grace: "the reason why God justifies us freely is, not that the Law does not point out perfect righteousness, but because we fail in keeping it" (cf. Leviticus 18:5; Romans 10:5; Galatians 3:12). The witnesses thus see Christ pressing the lawyer with his own words. The man had stated the standard perfectly; Christ bids him meet it — "this do, and thou shalt live." The command is true and just: perfect love would indeed bring life. But it is also unattainable by fallen man, and in laying it on the lawyer's conscience Christ exposes the futility of seeking life by doing. The very perfection of the law's demand is what shuts the self-righteous up to mercy — for none can do it, and to slip once is to fall short of the life it promises. Ellicott adds that in the command "this do" our Lord does more than accommodate himself to the legal view: love really was life, "at once its source and its manifestation, if only the love were true, and the test of its being true was action" — so the word both affirmed the law and probed the lawyer's loveless heart.
29:
"But he, willing to justify himself, said unto Jesus, And who is my neighbour?" The witnesses dwell on the lawyer's self-justification and the question it raised.

The question springs from wounded pride. Cyril: "the lawyer, when praised by our Saviour for having answered right, breaks forth into pride, thinking that he had no neighbour, as though there was no one to be compared to him in righteousness." His asking "who is my neighbour" itself betrays his lack of love: "he proves himself to be devoid of love for his neighbour, since he did not consider any one to be his neighbour, and consequently of the love of God; for he who loves not his brother whom he sees, cannot love God whom he does not see" (cf. 1 John 4:20).

Ambrose links his ignorance to unbelief: "he answered that he knew not his neighbour, because he believed not on Christ; and he who knows not Christ knows not the law." The lawyer, "willing to justify himself" (cf. Luke 16:15; Romans 4:5), sought to narrow the command's reach so that he might appear to have kept it.

Christ's coming parable will widen what the lawyer would narrow. Theophylact anticipates it: "our Saviour defines a neighbour not in respect of actions or honour, but of nature... all who partake of the same nature are your neighbours. Be thou also their neighbour, not in place, but in affection and solicitude." The witnesses thus see the lawyer's second question as the unmasking of his heart. Having been caught by "this do, and thou shalt live," he tries to escape by restricting the term "neighbour" — hoping that, with the circle drawn small enough, he might be found to have loved sufficiently. But the question, meant to justify him, only revealed how little he understood of love; and it gave Christ the occasion to answer, in the parable of the Good Samaritan, that the neighbour is anyone in need, and the duty of love as wide as humanity itself. Calvin notes the same self-justifying spirit lurks in every heart that would limit the law's demand to make its own obedience seem sufficient; the parable that follows leaves no room for such evasion, defining the neighbour so widely that none in need can be excluded from our love.
30:
"And Jesus answering said, A certain man went down from Jerusalem to Jericho, and fell among thieves, which stripped him of his raiment, and wounded him, and departed, leaving him half dead." The witnesses dwell on the dangerous road, the violence, and the deeper allegory.

The road was notorious. Lightfoot calls it "the most beaten and frequented road in the whole land of Israel," and explains the "great traffic between these two cities" by the priestly courses at both. Barnes describes the country as "rocky and mountainous... a convenient place for highwaymen," noting Josephus's account that Herod once "dismissed forty thousand men... a large part of whom became highwaymen." The "thieves" were robbers — Barnes: "highwaymen, and not merely took the property, but endangered the life."

The man is left "half dead" — stripped, wounded, helpless. Robertson and the grammatical commentators note the vivid realism: the victim can do nothing for himself, dependent wholly on whoever should pass (cf. Matthew 20:29).

Beneath the story the fathers read the fall of man. Augustine (on v. 29) takes "that man... for Adam himself, representing the race of man; Jerusalem, the city of peace, that heavenly country, from the bliss of which he fell; Jericho... signifies our mortality," that "rises, increases, wanes, and sets." Gill agrees the parable, beyond its plain lesson, represents "the sad estate and condition of mankind by the fall, and their recovery by Christ." The witnesses thus see in the wounded traveller both a real sufferer, whose plight will test the hearts of those who meet him, and a figure of fallen humanity — stripped of its first righteousness, wounded by sin, left half-dead on the road from the heavenly city, unable to save itself, and waiting for one who will show mercy (cf. Isaiah 1:6). Lightfoot notes the road was so travelled by priests and Levites because of the temple courses shared between Jerusalem and Jericho, which makes the appearance of a priest and a Levite upon it (vv. 31-32) wholly natural, and their failure of mercy all the more pointed. Barnes adds that Judea at that time "abounded with robbers", so that the scene Christ paints would have struck his hearers as wholly true to life, not a far-fetched supposition but the kind of tragedy that befell travellers on that very road.
31:
"And by chance there came down a certain priest that way: and when he saw him, he passed by on the other side." The witnesses dwell on the seeming chance, the priest, and his heartless evasion.

"By chance" the commentators take as coincidence with a deeper meaning. Robertson renders it "by way of coincidence"; Ellicott hears "a touch of irony": "it seemed so casual, as such opportunities always do to men who neglect them, and yet it was, in the purpose of God, the test-moment of each man's character and life." Clarke: "the coincidence of time and circumstance."

The priest had every reason to help and did not. Lightfoot and Ellicott note that Jericho was "a priestly city," so the priest was likely "returning from his week of sacerdotal offices"; Barnes adds that "not less than twelve thousand priests and Levites dwelt at Jericho." His office bound him to mercy — Clarke: "from the nature of their office, [they were] most obliged to perform works of mercy... their inhuman conduct here was a flat breach of the law" (cf. Deuteronomy 22:4).

Yet "he passed by on the other side." Ellicott names the likely excuses — "the trouble and peril of meddling with a man whom robbers had just attacked," and "the fear of incurring a ceremonial defilement by coming into contact with what might possibly be a corpse" — so he "passed by on the other side, not of the road only, but of the ravine." Lightfoot is blunter: "I would impute it wholly to the mere want of charity" (cf. Psalm 38:11). The witnesses thus see the first passer-by condemn himself by his very office. A minister of God, fresh from the temple's holy service, sees a fellow Israelite dying and turns aside — putting ceremonial scruple, or mere convenience, above the plain duty of mercy. The man whose whole calling was to mediate between God and men would not cross the road to save one of them. Ellicott notes the priest "passed by on the other side, not of the road only, but of the ravine through which the road passed" — putting the widest possible distance between himself and the dying man, as if to spare himself even the sight of a need he would not relieve.
32:
"And likewise a Levite, when he was at the place, came and looked on him, and passed by on the other side." The witnesses dwell on the Levite, his nearer look, and his deeper guilt.

The Levite, like the priest, was a minister of religion. Barnes describes the office: the Levites were "set apart to the duties of religion," to "render assistance to the priests," to keep the tabernacle and temple, prepare supplies, and conduct the sacred music (cf. Numbers 8; 1 Chronicles 23). He too, Ellicott notes, "may have been coming... from his week of service in the Temple." This is, Ellicott observes, "the only mention of Levites in the Gospels."

His conduct was at once better and worse than the priest's. The commentators mark a difference in the words. Barnes: "the expression used does not denote, as in the case of the priest, that he accidentally saw him and took no farther notice, but that he came and looked on him more attentively — but still did nothing to relieve him." Ellicott draws it out: the Levite is "better in that he does not altogether turn aside, but comes and looks; worse in that his second thoughts are at variance with his first, and prevail against them. If he has more light, he also sins more against it" (cf. Psalm 109:25).

The witnesses thus see in the Levite a still sharper rebuke. He came near, he looked, perhaps his heart was moved for a moment — and then he too passed by on the other side. His was not the careless neglect of one who barely noticed, but the deliberate hardening of one who saw, considered, and chose to do nothing. The nearer view made his refusal the more inexcusable; for he had felt the pull of pity and resisted it. Between them, priest and Levite — the two orders of Israel's ministry, the men of all others from whom mercy might be expected — left their countryman to die, and so condemned, by their example, a religion of office and form without the love that is its soul.
33:
"But a certain Samaritan, as he journeyed, came where he was: and when he saw him, he had compassion on him," The witnesses dwell on the unexpected helper, his compassion, and the figure of Christ.

The helper is the hated alien. Barnes: "the Samaritans were the most inveterate foes of the Jews... It was this fact which rendered the conduct of this good man so striking, set in strong contrast with the conduct of the priest and the Levite. They would not help their own afflicted countryman" (cf. John 4:9). Robertson marvels: "of all men in the world to do a neighbourly act!" Where the others "passed by," he "came where he was" — "came down upon him," not sidestepping but stopping in pity.

Ellicott notes the boldness of Christ's choice. He "singles out the Samaritan as a type of excellence, after his own recent repulse" by Samaritans (cf. Luke 9:53), and after being himself "reproached as being a Samaritan" (cf. John 8:48) — finding "true human feeling in one who outwardly was involved in heresy and schism," and preferring it "to the form of godliness without its power."

The fathers see Christ himself in the Samaritan. Gill: it may be Christ's design "to prove himself the best neighbour and friend of men, though he was traduced by the Jews as a Samaritan." Augustine (on v. 29): "in whom our Lord Jesus Christ would have himself typified, for Samaritan is interpreted to be keeper, and it is said... He shall not slumber nor sleep who keepeth Israel." The witnesses thus see the parable's turn: the one from whom no kindness could be expected proves the true neighbour, his compassion shaming the loveless ministers of Israel. And in him, the fathers add, is figured the Lord himself — the despised and rejected One who alone had compassion on fallen, wounded humanity, drawing near to save when priest and law had passed it by. Lightfoot observes that, by the schools' own teaching, the Jews would receive no courtesy from a Samaritan; yet our Lord makes the Samaritan the wounded man's neighbour, "thereby teaching us that even a stranger, yea, an enemy — against the doctrine of their own schools — is no other than our neighbour."
34:
"And went to him, and bound up his wounds, pouring in oil and wine, and set him on his own beast, and brought him to an inn, and took care of him." The witnesses dwell on the practical mercy, its cost, and its allegory.

The kindness is detailed and self-denying. Robertson notes the medical precision that "interested Luke" — "bound up his wounds" (a word "here only in the N.T."), "pouring on them oil and wine," the "household remedies even for wounds (soothing oil, antiseptic alcohol)." Barnes lists the whole: "he stopped — came where he was — pitied him — bound up his wounds — set him on his own beast — conducted him to a tavern... and all this without desiring or expecting any reward." Ellicott marks the cost: "his own beast (better, ass) is given up, and he goes on foot," the oil and wine "which had been provided for personal refreshment... freely given."

The inn the commentators distinguish. Clarke: "an inn (pandocheion), from pan, all, and dechomai, I receive, because it receives all comers." Ellicott notes it "implies the Western type of hostelry, where the landlord provides for his guests," unlike the bare caravanserai of Luke 2:7.

The fathers read the mercy as Christ's healing of the soul. Gill: "it being part of the work of Christ to bind up the broken-hearted, to heal wounded sinners" (cf. Isaiah 1:6; Psalm 147:3). The witnesses thus see in the Samaritan's care the very pattern of love, contrasted point by point with the priest's and Levite's neglect. He did not merely feel pity but acted on it, at real cost to himself — giving his oil, his wine, his beast, his time, his money. And in the allegory the fathers loved, the wounds bound and the oil and wine poured in figure the sacraments and graces by which Christ heals the sin-wounded soul and brings it to the inn of his Church, there to be cared for till he comes again. Clarke notes that oil and wine "beaten together appear to have been used formerly as a common medicine for fresh wounds", the wine cleansing and the oil soothing; the Samaritan used for another's healing the very provisions he had brought for his own journey, sparing nothing that might help.
35:
"And on the morrow when he departed, he took out two pence, and gave them to the host, and said unto him, Take care of him; and whatsoever thou spendest more, when I come again, I will repay thee." The witnesses dwell on the continued care, the generous provision, and the promised return.

The Samaritan's care did not end with first aid. He lodged with the man through the night, and on departing made provision for his continued keeping. The "two pence" — two denarii — Ellicott reckons "the average wages of a labourer for two days," "a sufficient and liberal provision for all probable contingencies"; Barnes and Robertson note the sum, small in figure, was "worth more in purchasing power" then.

His liberality went further still. "Whatsoever thou spendest more... I will repay thee." Ellicott: "this was not, in the Samaritan's judgment, enough, and he gave a carte blanche for whatever else might be required." Robertson notes the emphatic "I will repay" — "he was a man of his word and known to the innkeeper as reliable." Lightfoot notes the two pence answered to the half-shekel "ransom for his soul or life."

The fathers read the departure and promised return of Christ. Gill: "having taken care of him all night, and put him into a comfortable way, [he] leaves him, though in good hands; as Christ does his people, to learn to live by faith upon him." The "when I come again" they take of Christ's second coming. The witnesses thus see the Samaritan's mercy crowned by its thoroughness: he not only rescued the man but secured his ongoing care, paying now and pledging more, that nothing should be wanting. And in the allegory, the Lord, having healed the sin-wounded soul and committed it to the keeping of his Church, has gone away for a season, leaving ample provision, and will surely return to repay all that has been faithfully spent in the care of his own (cf. Luke 14:14; Proverbs 19:17). Ellicott notes the two pence, by the day-wage of Matthew 20:2, would more than cover the man's keep, yet the Samaritan would not have his charity limited by a sum, pledging the host whatever more the care might cost — mercy that counts no expense too great.
36:
"Which now of these three, thinkest thou, was neighbour unto him that fell among the thieves?" The witnesses dwell on the turning of the question and its disarming wisdom.

Christ reverses the lawyer's inquiry. Barnes: "the lawyer had asked him who was his neighbour? Jesus in this beautiful narrative showed him who and what a neighbour was." Robertson notes "Jesus has changed the lawyer's standpoint and has put it up to him to decide which of these three acted like a neighbour." The question is no longer "whom must I love?" but "who proved himself a lover?"

Ellicott marks the subtlety. The point was "as to whom the Jew should look on as his neighbour. It is answered indirectly by the narrative, which showed who had proved himself a neighbour to the Jew. The Samaritan had recognised a neighbour even in the Jew. The Jew therefore should recognise a neighbour even in the Samaritan." Thus the lawyer's narrow question is overturned: love does not first ask whether another qualifies as neighbour, but acts as neighbour to whoever is in need.

The parable disarms prejudice. Barnes: "had he at first told him that a Samaritan might be a neighbour to a Jew... he would have been at once revolted; but when, by a beautiful and affecting narrative, he brought the man himself to see that it might be, he was constrained to admit it. Here we see the beauty of a parable" — "it disarmed prejudice, fixed the attention... and prevented the possibility of cavil," like Nathan's parable to David (cf. 2 Samuel 12:7). The witnesses thus see Christ leading the hostile lawyer, by the sheer force of the story, to pronounce judgment against his own narrowness. He had hoped to limit the command; the parable widens it past all limiting. He cannot deny that the despised Samaritan was the true neighbour — and in admitting it, he condemns the loveless religion he had trusted and is brought, against his prejudice, to the verge of the truth. Ellicott notes there was something courageous as well as gracious in Christ's singling out a Samaritan for praise, so soon after a Samaritan village had refused him passage (Luke 9:53) and after his enemies had flung the name "Samaritan" at him as a reproach (John 8:48); he honours the truth wherever it is found.
37:
"And he said, He that shewed mercy on him. Then said Jesus unto him, Go, and do thou likewise." The witnesses dwell on the lawyer's forced confession and the command that closes the parable.

The lawyer answers truly, but cannot speak the name. Gill: "meaning the Samaritan; which he was obliged to declare, though of another country and religion... yet the case was so plain, as put by Christ, that he could not with any honour or conscience say otherwise." Clarke and Barnes note his lingering prejudice: "his Jewish prejudice would not permit him to name the Samaritan," but conscience "obliged him to acknowledge that he was the only righteous person of the three" — "he that shewed mercy."

The fathers fix on what makes a neighbour. Ambrose (on v. 29): "neither Priest nor Levite became neighbour to the sufferer, but he only who had compassion on him. For vain is the dignity of the Priesthood, and the knowledge of the Law, unless they are confirmed by good works"; "relationship does not make a neighbour, but compassion, for compassion is according to nature." Chrysostom: "if you see any one oppressed... be he Gentile or Jew and need help, dispute not; he has a claim to your assistance" (cf. Micah 6:8).

"Go, and do thou likewise" turns the lesson into a command. Barnes: "show the same kindness to all — to friend and foe — and then you will have evidence that you keep the law" (cf. Matthew 5:44; James 2:13). The witnesses thus see the parable end where the lawyer's pride began — but now humbled and instructed. He had asked, to justify himself, "who is my neighbour"; he is sent away with the answer that he must himself be neighbour to every one in need. The dignity of office and the knowledge of the law are nothing without mercy; and the proof of loving God is a love that, like the Samaritan's, crosses every barrier of race and religion to do good to whoever is in distress. The command is laid not on the lawyer only but on all who hear: go, and do thou likewise.
38:
"Now it came to pass, as they went, that he entered into a certain village: and a certain woman named Martha received him into her house." The witnesses dwell on the welcome given to Christ and the grace of his being received.

Bede sets the scene as the living counterpart to the parable: "the love of God and our neighbour, which was contained above in words and parables, is here set forth in very deed and reality." The village, Origen notes, is named by John as Bethany (cf. John 11:1).

The wonder of the welcome the fathers dwell on. Augustine: "the Lord, who came to his own, and his own received him not, was received as a guest... But a servant received her Lord, the sick her Saviour, the creature her Creator." Lest any envy that privilege, he adds Christ's word, "inasmuch as ye have done it to the least of my brethren, ye have done it to me" (cf. Matthew 25:40).
