It was, Augustine says, "by reason of his condescension, not his condition," that he who fed the angels "came himself to the feeder."

Cyril draws a lesson for guest and host alike: by his example Christ teaches his disciples, "when they come to a house," not "to remain idle, but rather fill the minds of those who receive them with sacred and divine teaching"; and those who "make ready the house" should "go to meet their guests gladly," that "they will be edified by the teaching" and "receive the reward of charity." The witnesses thus see in Martha's hospitality a true act of love to Christ, and in his accepting it a marvel of grace — the Creator stooping to be entertained by his creature. Yet, as the sequel shows, even this good work would need to be ordered aright, lest the service of Christ crowd out the hearing of him. Augustine adds that Christ, "taking the form of a servant, wished therein to be fed by servants, by reason of his condescension"; he who had angels minister to him in the wilderness now came to the one who would feed him, that Martha's very service might be an honour granted by his grace.
39:
"And she had a sister called Mary, which also sat at Jesus' feet, and heard his word." The witnesses dwell on Mary's posture, her discipleship, and her humility.

To sit at the feet was to take the place of a learner. Barnes: "this was the ancient posture of disciples or learners. They sat at the feet of their teachers — that is, beneath them, in a humble place"; "hence Paul is represented as brought up at the feet of Gamaliel" (cf. Acts 22:3; Luke 8:35). When Mary "sat at Jesus' feet, it means that she was a disciple of his... anxious to learn his doctrine." Robertson notes "heard" is imperfect: "she took her seat by the feet of Jesus and went on listening to his talk."

Chrysostom marks her devotion: "it is not said simply that she sat near Jesus, but at his feet, to show her diligence, steadfastness, and zeal in hearing, and the great reverence which she had for our Lord." Augustine adds the fruit of her lowliness: "as was her humility in sitting at his feet, so much the more did she receive from him. For the waters pour down to the lowest part of the valley, but flow away from the rising of the hill."

The witnesses note the consistency of character across the Gospels. Ellicott observes "the identity of character here and in the entirely independent narratives of John 11 and 12" — there too "Martha is active and conspicuous in serving; Mary, meditative and emotional, pouring her whole soul into one act of love." The witnesses thus see in Mary the model hearer. She seized the one opportunity the others would lose, taking the disciple's humble place and drinking in the words of Christ. Her sitting still was no idleness but the highest activity of the soul — for to receive the teaching of the Lord, with reverence and lowliness, is the better service, and the one that abides. Augustine adds that Mary "was absorbed in the sweetness of our Lord's words, and was feeding on them with the deepest affection", so that while Martha prepared a feast for the Lord, Mary was already feasting at the Lord's own table, fed by the bread of his teaching.
40:
"But Martha was cumbered about much serving, and came to him, and said, Lord, dost thou not care that my sister hath left me to serve alone? bid her therefore that she help me." The witnesses dwell on Martha's distraction, her good intent, and her hasty complaint.

"Cumbered" pictures a divided mind. Clarke: "perplexed... harassed with different cares and employments at the same time; one drawing one way, and another, another — a proper description of a worldly mind." Ellicott: "distracted; drawn hither and thither by conflicting cares." Yet the commentators defend her motive. Barnes: "there is no evidence that Martha had a worldly or covetous disposition. Her anxiety was to provide suitable entertainment for the Lord Jesus." Clarke: "all her anxiety was to provide... for our Lord and his disciples," and we should not "attribute earthly-mindedness to a woman whose character stands unimpeachable."

Her fault lay in the interruption and the complaint. Augustine (on v. 38): "Martha, when much troubled in her occupation, interrupted our Lord, and complained of her sister." Barnes calls "dost thou not care" "an improper reproof of our Lord, as if he encouraged Mary in neglecting her duty." Ellicott notes the Greek "implies something like a hasty movement to interrupt the calm tenor of the Lord's discourse." Her request "that she help me" — Clarke explains the word as of "two pillars meeting at the top... mutually supporting each other."

The witnesses thus see Martha in a sympathetic but cautionary light. Her service was loving and her labour real; her sin was not in serving but in letting the service so distract and fret her that she begrudged her sister the better choice, and even ventured to chide the Lord. The good work, pursued without measure, had become a snare — robbing her of the peace Mary enjoyed at Jesus' feet, and betraying her into impatience with both her sister and her Guest (cf. 1 Corinthians 7:35). Augustine sets the two side by side: "the one was labouring, the other at rest"; Martha "was preparing a feast for our Lord, in whose feast Mary was now rejoicing" — and her fear, he suggests, was lest the Lord bid Mary rise and help her, the very thing he would not do.
41:
"And Jesus answered and said unto her, Martha, Martha, thou art careful and troubled about many things:" The witnesses dwell on the tender reproof and the anxiety it names.

The repeated name breathes love. Ellicott: "we note a special tenderness of reproof in the two-fold utterance of the name, of which this and the like iteration of Simon, Simon (cf. Luke 22:31) are the only examples in our Lord's recorded utterances" (compare also "Saul, Saul"). Wesley: "there is a peculiar spirit and tenderness in the repetition of the word." Augustine (on v. 38): "the repetition of the name is a mark of love, or perhaps of drawing the attention, that she should listen more earnestly." The better text, Ellicott notes, reads "the Lord answered."

The words name a double distress. "Careful," Robertson explains, is the verb (merimnais) "for worry and anxiety... to be divided, distracted," the very word of the Sermon on the Mount, "Take no thought" (cf. Matthew 6:25). "Troubled" (thorybazē) adds the outward agitation: "Martha had both inward anxiety and outward agitation." Wesley sums it: "thou art careful, inwardly, and hurried, outwardly."

The reproof, the witnesses observe, is gentle but real. Christ does not condemn Martha's love or even her serving, but the anxious, distracted, agitated spirit into which her serving had drawn her — the being "troubled about many things." It is the same disease he had warned against on the mount: the heart divided among many cares, robbed of peace by the very abundance of its concerns. The witnesses thus hear in "Martha, Martha" the voice of a friend, not a judge — one who loves her enough to call her by name and turn her from the fretfulness of much serving to the calm of the one thing that matters, which her sister had already chosen. Robertson notes the verb for "careful" is the same Jesus used repeatedly in the Sermon on the Mount and elsewhere against anxious worry; Martha's fault was thus no new thing but the very care about "many things" that Christ had again and again warned divides and distracts the heart from God.
42:
"But one thing is needful: and Mary hath chosen that good part, which shall not be taken away from her." The witnesses dwell on the one thing needful, Mary's choice, and its permanence.

The "one thing needful" the witnesses take of the soul's true business. Gill rejects "too low a sense," that one dish was meant (though "some ancient writers have given into" it), reading it of "the hearing of the word... necessary to the knowledge of Christ, and salvation by him." Barnes: "that is, religion, or piety. This is eminently and peculiarly needful. Other things are of little importance" (cf. 1 Timothy 4:8; Matthew 6:33). Augustine (on v. 38) draws the contrast: "there are many, diverse things, which are carnal, temporal; but one is preferred to many. For one is not from many, but many from one." (Robertson notes a textual variant, "few things, or one," but the sense holds.)

Mary's choice is the "good part" that endures. Barnes: "the portion of the gospel; the love of God, and an interest in his kingdom. She had chosen to be a Christian." And it "shall not be taken away" — "God will not take away his grace from his people, neither shall any man pluck them out of his hand" (cf. John 10:28; Psalm 27:4).

Barnes draws the lessons. "The cares of this life are dangerous, even when they seem most lawful" — even providing for Christ, when it engrossed the mind, drew Martha's reproof. "It is of more importance to attend to the instructions of the Lord Jesus than to be engaged in the affairs of the world... The one will abide for ever; the other will be but for a little time." And piety, he adds, is the chief ornament, sweetening every grace. The witnesses thus see Christ gently reordering Martha's values, and ours. He does not despise her service, but ranks it below the better part — for the meal she prepared would be eaten and forgotten, while the word Mary received would never be taken from her. The lesson is for all: in the press of many lawful cares, to secure first the one thing needful, and choose, with Mary, the part that abides forever.
